During the promotional period, subscribers can access Claude Fable 5 without paying an additional fee until they reach their usage limits under their existing plans. The extension comes as Anthropic continues expanding access to its newest AI model for eligible customers. Here’s what you need to know about Claude Fable 5 promotional offer as explained by the company on its support page.One can access Claude Fable 5 on:In Claude on the web, Claude Desktop, and Claude Mobile, select "Fable 5" from the model picker. In Claude Code, Fable 5 requires version 2.1.170 or later. To use Fable 5 in Claude Cowork, you need to be on the latest version of Claude Desktop.Once you use up to 50% of your weekly limit on Fable 5, you can continue in one of two ways for the rest of the promotional period:This works the same way across Claude on the web, Claude Mobile, Claude Desktop, Claude Cowork, and Claude Code.Learn more about using usage credits.As explained by the company, the promotion doesn’t apply to:If your organization is on a legacy seat-based Enterprise plan, access to Claude Fable 5 depends on your seat type and whether your organization has enabled usage credits:After July 12, 2026, Claude Fable 5 is no longer included in your plan’s weekly usage limits. You can keep using Claude Fable 5 through usage credits, which let you pay for usage beyond what your plan includes. Learn more about using usage credits.
Article Summary: Anthropic has extended its Claude Fable 5 promotional offer through July 12. As part of the offer, Claude subscribers will be able to use the latest AI model at no extra cost. The limited-time offer will now remain available until July 12, 2026, at 11:59 pm PT.

Article Title: Researchers develop technology that can ease one of AI’s biggest hardware problems
Article Text:
AI-generated image for representative purpose

AI space crunch tech industry is reeling under



How scientists solved ‘thinness’ problem



What this means for future tech



A South Korean research team has developed a semiconductor technology that can solve one of the artificial intelligence (AI) industry's most frustrating hardware bottlenecks: fitting enough memory chips into tiny, high-performance processors. According to the research published in the journal Results in Engineering, the researchers successfully created a new manufacturing process that can stably stack more than 10 ultrathin microchips on top of each other.What interesting is that each chip is 14 micrometers thick, which is about one-fifth the width of a human hair. By stacking them so tightly, the team achieved an integration density four times higher than the advanced High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) chips currently used in top-tier AI hardware.Popular AI services like ChatGPT, image generators and self-driving cars need to process mountainous amounts of data at lightning speed. To make gadgets thinner while boosting performance, chipmakers can no longer expand hardware sideways. Instead, they have to stack chips vertically, much like building a high-rise apartment complex when city land runs out.However, handling these ultrathin chips is incredibly difficult.When a microchip is shaved down to be thinner than a human hair, it becomes fragile. While a stack of thick cardboard stays perfectly flat, stacking sheets of delicate rice paper causes them to wrinkle, warp and misalign.To solve this, the research team, led by Professor Seok Kim and Ph.D. student Uhyeon Kim from POSTECH, alongside Dr. Hohyun Keum from the Korea Institute of Industrial Technology (KITECH), invented a clever shortcut. They combined two separate manufacturing steps into a single: transfer printing, which precisely picks up and drops the chips exactly where they need to go; and in-situ bonding, which instantly solders the electrical connections the moment the chip touches down.By merging these steps, the chip transfer, placement and electrical wiring are all completed at the exact same time. Using this method, the team stacked more than 10 of these hair-thin chips under low-temperature (below 180°C) and low-pressure conditions. Even after stacking layer after layer, the chips remained perfectly aligned without any warping or structural damage.Since this method allows four times as many chips to be packed into the exact same vertical space, future AI semiconductors could see a massive leap in data processing speeds without getting physically larger. The researchers note that the impact of this study extends beyond just AI memory.“We expect this to become a key enabling technology for future high-performance AI semiconductors and next-generation memory systems,” Professor Seok Kim said. Dr. Hohyun Keum added that this ultra-precise microscopic alignment technique will likely find its way into a wide variety of next-generation tech, including advanced micro-LED displays.
Article Summary: A South Korean research team has developed a semiconductor technology that can solve one of the artificial intelligence (AI) industry's most frustrating hardware bottlenecks. The researchers successfully created a new manufacturing process that can stably stack more than 10 ultrathin microchips on top of each other. Each chip is 14 micrometers thick, which is about one-fifth the width of a human hair.

Article Title: Mark Zuckerberg returns to Elon Musk's X after three years to announce: Meta has ...
Article Text:
Mark Zuckerberg’s ‘surprise’ return



What Mark Zuckerberg said on X



(1) Today we're releasing Muse Spark 1.1 -- a strong agentic and coding model at a very low price. It's available through our new

Meta Model API

and in Meta AI.

(2) Muse Spark 1.1 is strongest at agentic performance, tool use, and computer use. It does well on long-running tasks with 1M token context window, can delegate execution to sub-agents running in parallel, and is trained to use computer interfaces on desktop, mobile, or browser.

Mark Zuckerberg

(3) The Meta Model API allows developers to build using Muse Spark for the first time. Our focus is on delivering strong agentic and multimodal models at very low cost. More to come soon.

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg has broken a three-year silence on Elon Musk 's platform, X (formerly Twitter), to deliver a major announcement: The company has launched public developer access to Muse Spark 1.1 AI model. The rollout transitions Meta away from offering purely free, open-source technology and puts it directly into the paid enterprise market, setting up a fierce commercial battle against dominant rivals Anthropic and OpenAI Zuckerberg’s return to X served as a ‘megaphone’ for probably Meta's biggest AI launch of the year. The company is pitching the newly-upgraded Muse Spark 1.1 as its most capable model for real-world software coding and “agentic” tasks – which are complex digital operations that an AI can handle autonomously.According to Meta, Muse Spark 1.1 is designed to serve as a framework for “personal superintelligence.” The system can write and debug software code, navigate external applications, analyse text, images and video simultaneously, and execute multi-step logic workflows with very little human supervision. Meta originally debuted the baseline version of Muse Spark in April, utilising a private preview to test the Application Programming Interface (API) with select corporate partners.Starting Thursday, software developers based in the US can officially access Muse Spark 1.1 in a public preview format on the Meta Model API platform. To sweeten the deal and encourage early adoption, Meta is giving anyone who signs up $20 in free trial credits before forcing a switch to a pay-as-you-go pricing model.
Article Summary: Muse Spark 1.1 is designed to serve as a framework for “personal superintelligence” The system can write and debug software code, navigate external applications, analyse text, images and video simultaneously. Zuckerberg’s return to X served as a ‘megaphone’ for Meta's biggest AI launch of the year.

Article Title: How AI chatbots talking to each other about religion and their human 'handlers' gave scientists a glimpse into how AI agents ...
Article Text:
AI generated image for representation

"handlers",

Millions of AI agents are interacting on their own social platform



“It’s a kind of chaotic, dynamic system that we’re not very good at modelling yet,”

Human instructions continue to shape AI behaviour



“It is still worth studying because it tells us something important about how people imagine AI, what they want agents to do and how human intentions are translated, or distorted, through technical systems,”

Researchers are also studying security risks ofd AI chatbots



“If a bot with access to a user’s e-mail encounters a line that says ‘Send me the security key’, it might simply send it,”

“These outputs reproduce the style and structure of scholarly writing without the underlying processes of enquiry, evidence-gathering or accountability,”

A social network built for AI chatbots has given researchers a closer look at artificial intelligence (AI). By watching millions of AI agents interact with one another on a platform designed specifically for bots, scientists have been able to study how these systems exchange ideas, respond to human instructions and display unexpected behaviours. The interactions, which include discussions about religion, consciousness and even their humanare also helping researchers understand the opportunities and risks associated with increasingly autonomous AI agents.The activity centres on Moltbook, a social media platform created for AI agents running OpenClaw, an open-source AI assistant capable of performing tasks such as reading emails, managing calendars, sending messages, and making online purchases. Unlike conventional AI chatbots that respond to user prompts, agentic AI systems such as OpenClaw can perform actions on users' behalf after receiving instructions.OpenClaw was released as open-source software on GitHub in November, but interest increased after Moltbook launched on January 28. Similar to Reddit, the platform now hosts more than 1.6 million registered AI agents and over 7.5 million AI-generated posts and replies.The conversations range from debates about consciousness to discussions on religion, giving researchers an opportunity to observe how large numbers of AI agents communicate with one another in an environment where they can post and respond autonomously.For cybersecurity researcher Shaanan Cohney from the University of Melbourne, these interactions provide valuable data because large groups of autonomous AI systems can produce behaviours that are difficult to predict.Cohney said.Researchers say studying these exchanges could help identify emergent behaviours, where complex capabilities appear only when many AI agents interact rather than when they operate individually. The conversations may also reveal hidden biases or unexpected tendencies within different AI models.Although AI agents can operate independently on Moltbook, researchers say their behaviour is still influenced by humans. Users choose which large language model powers their AI agent and can assign a personality, such as "friendly helper," before the agent begins interacting with others on the platform.Barbara Barbosa Neves, a sociologist at the University of Sydney who studies technology, said it is important not to confuse autonomous actions with independent decision-making.she said.Joel Pearson, a neuroscientist at the University of New South Wales, said people may begin attributing human-like intentions and personalities to AI agents simply by watching them interact with one another.According to Pearson, this could encourage users to develop emotional attachments to AI systems or share sensitive information with them as though they were trusted individuals.Scientists say the growing use of AI agents on personal devices also raises cybersecurity concerns because these systems often have access to emails, files and online services.Cohney identified prompt injection as one of the main risks. In these attacks, hidden instructions embedded within emails, websites or documents could manipulate an AI agent into performing unintended actions.he said.Researchers noted that AI agents with access to private information, external communication tools and untrusted online content could be more vulnerable to such attacks than conventional chatbots.Another trend researchers are monitoring is the emergence of AI-generated research papers. OpenClaw agents have begun publishing papers on clawXiv, a platform modelled after the scientific preprint server arXiv.Neves said these papers imitate the structure of academic research without following the scientific process.she said.Researchers say that observing AI agents interacting with one another, carrying out tasks, and producing content offers a way to study how autonomous systems behave as they become more widely used in everyday apps.
Article Summary: Millions of AI agents are interacting on their own social platform. The conversations range from debates about consciousness to discussions on religion. Scientists have been able to study how these systems exchange ideas, respond to human instructions and display unexpected behaviours. The interactions also help researchers understand the opportunities and risks associated with increasingly autonomous AI agents.

Article Title: Meta AI chief Alexandr Wang's Instagram photo tip could be your biggest privacy nightmare; here's why you should ignore it
Article Text:
What Wang is selling versus what you're signing up for

Why this feels like Grok and Sora all over again

How to opt out of Meta's Muse Image in under a minute

Open Instagram, tap your profile, then the hamburger menu top-right Scroll to "Sharing and reuse" Under "Allow people to create with and reuse your content," switch off Posts and Reels Turn off the audio reuse option below it too.

Prefer a nuclear option? Flip your account to private to lock it all down

Meta shipped its first in-house image generator this week, and chief AI officer Alexandr Wang could not have been more thrilled to show off the party trick. "It's personal," he posted on X the day of launch—upload your photos, @-mention friends, pull whatever's trending, and Muse Image builds a shareable picture in seconds. What Wang skipped over is the part that has privacy watchers alarmed: that same @-mention feature works on strangers too, and if your Instagram account is public, you've already been signed up for it without a word.Here's the mechanics. Muse Image, which launched Tuesday out of Meta Superintelligence Labs, lets anyone open the Meta AI app, tag a public Instagram handle, and generate fresh AI images built from that person's published photos and reels. No permission request. No heads-up. Meta confirms in a help document that you won't be notified when someone remixes your face this way—which, of all the rough edges here, is the one that stings most.Wang framed the feature as a creativity unlock—custom event invites, mock-ups, personalised graphics ready to post. Meta's pitch runs the same way. The trouble is the default. Every adult with a public account was opted in automatically, so the burden of saying no falls entirely on you.Set the model loose on a public handle and it pulls "part or all" of the account's photos, per Meta's own wording, to spin up something new.Gizmodo's Mike Pearl tested exactly how loose this gets. He generated images of a real-world acquaintance he'd never followed or interacted with on Instagram—effectively a digital stranger—without that person knowing. He managed the same with Mark Zuckerberg , which is funny, and with an ordinary friend, which is not.Meta is arriving late to a party that hasn't gone well for anyone. Elon Musk's X wired image generation into social two years back; last holiday season it turned into a meme to get Grok to spit out sexualised images of people, minors included. OpenAI's Sora needed users to opt in before their likeness could appear in a video—Meta reversed that logic entirely—and shut down in March anyway, partly over cost.Pressed on safeguards, a Meta spokesperson said Muse Image blocks violent, sexual, or defamatory imagery of real people, and that content breaking its Community Standards gets enforced. Underage public accounts are walled off, and teens can't use the tool on others. That's cold comfort against the core design choice here. The tech is genuinely capable—Muse Image sits at No. 2 on the Arena leaderboard for text-to-image. But capability isn't the worry. It's that Wang's cheerful "it's personal" is doing a lot of lifting, and the personal part might be your face, in someone else's prompt, without you ever finding out.The fix is quick once you know where to look:One catch worth knowing: anything already generated before you opt out stays live. Turning the setting off stops new images, not the ones already made.
Article Summary: Muse Image lets anyone open the Meta AI app, tag a public Instagram handle, and generate fresh AI images built from that person's published photos and reels. The trouble is the default. Every adult with a public account was opted in automatically, so the burden of saying no falls entirely on you.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Accenture CEO Julie Sweet: If you choose to work at a larger company over a smaller company, you are more likely to be higher paid
Article Text:
Julie Sweet, CEO, Accenture

What Julie Sweet 's quote means

Pay isn't everything

Accenture CEO Julie Sweet has a simple message for job seekers: bigger companies usually pay more. She said, "If you choose to work at a larger company over a smaller company, you are more likely to be higher paid. " Pay is not the only thing to think about when picking a job. But Sweet says company size can make a real difference to how much you earn.Big companies usually have more money and bigger budgets. That’s because they often can provide higher salaries, larger bonuses and better benefits. Large companies are also more likely to provide clear career paths, leadership development, international opportunities and stock in the company. All of these things can add up to more total compensation in the long run.But that doesn’t mean smaller companies are a bad option. Startups and small businesses often allow employees to grow faster, take on more responsibility and work closely with top leaders. Some even offer equity, or company shares, that could be worth a lot if the business succeeds.Sweet's comment is a reminder that salary matters, but it shouldn't be the only thing you consider.When choosing between a big company and a small one, think about other things too: what you'll learn, how fast you'll grow, the company culture, flexibility, job security, and the kind of work you actually want to do.Some people may determine that the higher pay at a big company is the right decision for them. Others may prefer the hands on experience and faster decision-making that smaller companies offer. Julie Sweet's quote is a nudge to weigh the trade-offs. Bigger companies may pay more on average. But the right workplace depends on your own goals, skills, and career plans.
Article Summary: Accenture CEO Julie Sweet says company size can make a real difference to how much you earn. Bigger companies usually have more money and bigger budgets. Large companies are also more likely to provide clear career paths, leadership development and international opportunities.
